title: Agentic AI Will Make the CMO’s Role More Consequential
gist: As AI agents become active participants in the buying journey, marketing must sell to both humans and machines, forcing brand promise and operational performance into alignment. BCG argues this new class of AI-interaction data can elevate the CMO from an efficiency-focused role into a strategic engine for enterprise growth.

themes.theme: Agentic commerce and the dual decision-maker

insights.claim: Commerce is shifting to an 'agentic' model where AI agents become active participants in the customer journey from intent to purchase, forcing companies to account for two decision makers: humans and the AI models that influence their choices.
insights.speaker: Lauren Taylor
insights.so_what: Marketing strategy must now be built for machine evaluation in parallel with human persuasion; brands optimized only for human attention risk being filtered out before a person ever sees them.
insights.quote: traditional commerce shifts to a model in which AI agents become additional and active participants in customers’ journey from intent to purchase
insights.locator: Customers are increasingly turning to AI

insights.claim: AI interactions expose customers' underlying motivations, goals, and preferences—not just the intent revealed in a search query—creating a new class of customer intelligence beyond traditional data sources.
insights.speaker: Lauren Taylor
insights.so_what: The richest proprietary signal now lives inside AI conversations; firms that can capture and act on this data gain a durable insight advantage over rivals relying on clicks and categories.
insights.locator: As AI increasingly intermediates between brands and customers

insights.claim: Being visible to AI is different from being credibly recommended by AI, because agents evaluate trustworthiness and compare alternatives based on observable performance rather than persuasive messaging.
insights.speaker: Lauren Taylor
insights.so_what: Investment in AI visibility (structured data, APIs) is table stakes; the real competitive battle is for the agent's endorsement, which rewards operational reality over marketing spend.
insights.quote: The competition is not just for brands to be included in the answer, but to be the option the AI chooses to endorse.
insights.locator: When CMOs think about AI-driven discovery

insights.claim: Nearly half of responses from major AI platforms contain unsolicited information such as additional rationale, comparisons, and recommendations the user never requested, per research from Profound.
insights.speaker: Lauren Taylor
insights.so_what: AI proactively steers buyers toward or away from brands, so a weak comparative position can cost sales even when a customer never asked for alternatives.
insights.quote: nearly half of responses from major AI platforms contain unsolicited information

insights.claim: The same APIs and structured data feeds that make a brand findable will increasingly make its performance measurable, both internally and against competitors, exposing gaps between brand promise and delivery.
insights.speaker: Lauren Taylor
insights.so_what: Companies with weak fulfillment, pricing integrity, or service resolution face structural downgrading by agents; operational quality becomes a marketing asset investors can partly assess via reviews and complaint patterns.
insights.locator: The same APIs and structured data feeds

insights.claim: A financial services firm positioned on trust can have its brand promise undermined if AI agents detect patterns of high claim rejection rates, slow resolution, or excessive documentation requirements.
insights.speaker: Lauren Taylor
insights.so_what: Sectors with visible service-quality complaints (insurers, telcos, banks) are most exposed to agent-driven reputation arbitrage; brand narrative can no longer paper over operational shortfalls.
insights.locator: A financial services company may position itself on trust

insights.claim: Because AI systems combine products and services across multiple categories to meet an expressed need, they reveal patterns of co-mingled demand that traditional research rarely uncovers, so competition now extends beyond product silos.
insights.speaker: Lauren Taylor
insights.so_what: Category-defined market maps understate competitive threat; incumbents may lose to adjacent technologies serving the same customer outcome, reshaping how addressable markets should be sized.
insights.quote: What appears as separate markets through the lens of products often emerges as a single customer outcome through the lens of intent.

insights.claim: Google's AI Mode data shows queries are now triple the length of traditional searches, with the fastest-growing query type containing decision-oriented language such as 'which one' and 'which of.'
insights.speaker: Lauren Taylor
insights.so_what: Consumers are moving decision-making itself into AI channels the brand does not own, meaning influence must be won upstream and off-platform rather than at the point of sale.
insights.quote: queries are now triple the length of traditional searches
insights.locator: Google’s data on AI Mode shows

insights.claim: New AI-interaction intelligence can directly influence what a company builds—informing product development, bundling, partnerships, and new business models—not just how it markets what it already sells.
insights.speaker: Lauren Taylor
insights.so_what: Marketing shifts upstream into strategy and R&D; the CMO's data becomes a source of new-product and business-model direction, expanding the function's claim on enterprise value.
insights.locator: What’s new isn’t simply more data

insights.claim: Many companies have a 'customer-shaped hole' at the center of their strategy not from lack of data but from lacking the organizational wiring to translate consumer intelligence into decisions, hindered by siloed data, disconnected incentives, and intelligence that arrives too late.
insights.speaker: Lauren Taylor
insights.so_what: The binding constraint is organizational, not technological, so the winners will be firms that redesign decision plumbing—an execution gap that separates leaders from laggards regardless of AI spend.
insights.quote: many companies have a customer-shaped hole at the center of their strategy
insights.locator: Yet many companies have a customer-shaped hole

themes.theme: Customer experience and the end of the attention economy

insights.claim: The two-decade-old 'attention economy' assumption that a human with a short attention span is always on the other end no longer holds uniformly, as journeys become increasingly agent-mediated or agent-enhanced.
insights.speaker: Lauren Taylor
insights.so_what: Advertising and behavioral-nudge playbooks optimized for human psychology lose potency when a machine is the evaluator; ad-dependent business models face structural pressure.
insights.quote: That assumption no longer holds uniformly.

insights.claim: 43% of consumer journeys are now research-led with no predetermined brand choice, the average consumer encounters more than 15 touchpoints before purchase (up from roughly 5 a decade ago).
insights.speaker: Lauren Taylor
insights.so_what: Brand loyalty is eroding into open comparison shopping, raising customer-acquisition complexity and rewarding brands that can win contested, multi-touchpoint evaluations.
insights.quote: The average consumer now encounters more than 15 touchpoints before purchasing, up from roughly 5 a decade ago.
insights.locator: BCG research underscores the scale of this shift

insights.claim: Among consumers who use AI tools in research, 85% rank them among their top five most influential purchase touchpoints—on par with social media and well ahead of traditional media—while BCG's Global Consumer Radar shows overall GenAI usage up 40% in just over a year.
insights.speaker: Lauren Taylor
insights.so_what: AI has already become a top-tier influence channel; shopping-related GenAI use is among the fastest-growing applications, a tailwind for AI-commerce infrastructure and a threat to legacy media budgets.
insights.quote: 85% rank them among their top five most influential touchpoints in the purchase decision
insights.locator: BCG research underscores the scale of this shift

insights.claim: What makes a proprietary agent attractive to a brand does not necessarily make it attractive to a customer, since buyers comparing across categories trust neutral agents more than those tied to a single provider.
insights.speaker: Lauren Taylor
insights.so_what: Most branded-agent bets will fail to earn customer preference; the risk of disintermediating existing channels makes it a board-level decision, not a default AI initiative.
insights.quote: what makes an agent attractive to a brand does not necessarily make it attractive to a customer
insights.locator: In a world where AI increasingly mediates

insights.claim: Branded agents are most likely to earn genuine customer preference only in categories where trust and privacy matter deeply or where long-term relationships create meaningful advantages, such as a bank with a customer's financial history.
insights.speaker: Lauren Taylor
insights.so_what: Points to which incumbents (banks, healthcare, high-trust relationships) can defensibly own the agent layer versus those that should stay on neutral platforms.
insights.locator: Customers making decisions across multiple categories

themes.theme: Operating model and the strategic CMO

insights.claim: Only 14% of CEOs and CFOs consider their CMO highly effective at driving market growth (Gartner), yet 96% of nearly 300 global CMOs in BCG's survey say AI is driving end-to-end transformation of their function, with only about a third having moved beyond the basics.
insights.speaker: Lauren Taylor
insights.so_what: A credibility gap plus early-stage AI adoption means most marketing functions are unprepared; the two-thirds still on 'basics' represent both a laggard risk and a value-creation runway.
insights.quote: only 14% of CEOs and CFOs consider their CMO highly effective at driving market growth

insights.claim: Winning organizations must move from channel-based structures optimized for campaign execution to intelligence-led operating models where insight flows across functions toward the singular outcome of growth.
insights.speaker: Lauren Taylor
insights.so_what: Redesigning the paid/owned/earned silo structure is the prerequisite for monetizing agentic data; org structure is the differentiator, not tooling.
insights.locator: The marketing organizations best positioned to win

insights.claim: Most CMOs are focused on applying AI to productivity, but the greater opportunity lies in a new class of AI-interaction data that can build stronger brands, reveal where new value can be created, and establish marketing as essential to enterprise strategy.
insights.speaker: Lauren Taylor
insights.so_what: Framing the productivity-versus-growth split identifies where outsized returns sit; first-movers who treat AI data as strategic advantage rather than a cost-cutting tool will define the category.
insights.quote: The interaction between customers and AI will generate a new class of data that is richer and more revealing than anything traditional marketing analytics has produced.
insights.locator: Most CMOs, like most business leaders
